data.title: Silver Is On The Move - And Pan American Silver Is One Of The Cleanest Ways To Play It
data.description:
Since I last covered Pan American Silver (PAAS) (PAAS:CA) with a Buy rating, shares are up 30.41% on a total return basis—with silver prices surging in recent weeks.

In my prior article, I outlined why silver appeared to be setting up for a meaningful move higher as it completed a multi-year cup-and-handle formation. With that thesis now playing out, a conservative price target would be 0.75 x the cup depth projected above the breakout level—which gets me to a price target of $82 for silver.

PAAS has surged along with silver, but its recent strong performance isn't just about the silver rally. Operational improvements and high-quality assets mean that PAAS is well placed to capture silver upside more efficiently than many peers. In this article I will explain why PAAS remains attractively valued, highly correlated to silver prices and why I continue to view it as a Buy despite recent gains.

Quarterly Production Trends

Silver production bounced back nicely in Q3'25, reaching 5.46 million ounces—a 7% increase from the previous quarter—thanks in large part to the addition of the Juanicipio mine, where PAAS holds a 44% stake. Juanicipio's contribution helped offset weaker performance at some of the older mines: Dolores continued its gradual decline, and output at Minera Florida more than halved compared to Q2. La Colorada stayed steady at around 1.5 million ounces, reinforcing its status as the workhorse in the portfolio, while Cerro Moro showed a modest recovery after a slow first half. Overall, year-over-year silver production was basically flat, but the standout metric is definitely costs: all-in-sustaining costs fell sharply to $15.43 per ounce.

The trend for gold production has been lower. Production in Q3 edged up to 183,500 ounces but still sits below last year's levels. Drops at Timmins and Dolores outweighed gains at Jacobina and Shahuindo. Costs also jumped to $1,697 per ounce.

Q3 Earnings

PAAS reported Q3 earnings on the 12th of November—delivering record free cash flow with the first tangible results from Juanicipio already contributing meaningfully. Even though the MAG Silver acquisition, which closed on the 4th of September, contributed only a single month of production, it was enough to bring down silver costs, boost margins, and give management enough confidence to raise its full-year silver production guidance to 22-22.5 million ounces (from 21.5-22 million) and trim ASIC guidance to $14.50-$16 per ounce (from $15-$16.50).

I see Juanicipio as a true cornerstone for Pan American Silver for years to come. The mine's current plan covers roughly 13 years of production, but only a fraction of the property has been fully drilled, and there is plenty of room to extend its life further. With high grades, low costs, and strong cash flow, Juanicipio is already boosting Pan American Silver's margins, and over time, I think it could become a key engine of growth in one of the world's richest silver regions.

Even after the MAG Silver acquisition, PAAS is still in strong shape heading into the end of the year. PAAS closed Q3 with $910.8 million cash on hand, plus another $85.8 million from its 44% stake in Juanicipio—making cash on hand more than total outstanding debt.

Project Updates

At La Colorada, recent high-grade discoveries are creating exciting growth opportunities. PAAS is evaluating a two-phase plan for the Skarn project: an initial high-grade, lower-tonnage phase designed to generate early cash flow with modest capital requirements, followed by a larger-scale cave mining phase that could significantly expand production. By running the existing vein mine in parallel with both skarn phases, management thinks it can optimise infrastructure and extract maximum value from the deposit over time. An updated technical report is expected in the second quarter of 2026 and will include a preliminary economic assessment of this proposed approach.

At Jacobina, PAAS invested $18.9 million in Q3 to improve reliability, safety, and support long-term growth. Plant upgrades, optimisation studies and accelerated underground development aim to improve efficiency, reduce costs, and position the mine for better silver production in the years ahead.

Investors were left somewhat disappointed, as the Q3 earnings offered no new positive updates on Escobal. Management remarked:

Following the July 29, 2025 meeting between the Guatemalan Ministry of Energy and Mines ("MEM") and the Xinka Parliament ("XP") to address concerns described in a May 2025 statement issued by the XP, the MEM has held several, separate working meetings with the ministries involved in the ILO 169 consultation process, representatives from the XP, and the Company. The MEM has also made several appointments of key personnel to oversee and continue activities for the Escobal consultation process. The MEM has not provided a timeline for the completion of the ILO 169 consultation and there is no date for the restart of the Escobal mine

Risks

PAAS has fully benefited from strong production recently as well as the surge in silver prices. That said, I still think that some risks remain. Legacy mines will continue facing gradual declines, and the growth projects to replace those ounces carry typical execution risks—after all, it is still mining, even if lately it has felt almost like a cash-printing machine. Silver prices have also been very volatile of late, and any pullback could quickly pressure margins, cash flow, and investor sentiment. Currency risk is also very relevant for PAAS as it operates in multiple countries (Mexico, Peru, Argentina, Canada, and Brazil) while reporting in USD.

Valuation

I have selected Hecla, First Majestic Silver, Coeur, Endeavour Silver, Fresnillo, and Hochschild as comparables for PAAS because they all operate in the silver and precious metals space, with similar production profiles and exposure to key mining regions in the Americas. While there are some differences in scale, geographic focus, and metal mix, I think these companies provide a relevant benchmark for relative valuation.

I prefer using FWD EV/EBITDA ratios to value mining companies, as this tells me what I am paying for the underlying operations relative to the earnings they are forecast to generate.

Over the past two years, PAAS traded at an FWD EV/EBITDA ratio that was 3% above peers, on average. Currently, PAAS is trading at a ratio that is 5% below that of its peers, suggesting that shares are about 8% undervalued on a relative basis (or 0.7 standard deviations).

The Technical Take

PAAS shares are exhibiting strong momentum—and is trading well above the 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day moving averages, with a MACD indicator that is also still firmly in bullish territory. While the upward trend may have further room to run, I believe the current technical setup makes this a less than ideal entry point. Personally, I would wait for a pullback before increasing my position.

Takeaway

I maintain my Buy rating on Pan American Silver because I remain bullish on silver, and PAAS is one of the most direct ways to play further upside.

Pan American Silver's high-quality assets, low-cost profile, and recent production gains mean that it is well positioned to capture silver rallies efficiently, while operational improvements are also expected to support margins going forward.

Since my focus is on capturing a further rally in silver, I'm not setting a specific price target for PAAS shares. Instead, I am targeting $82 for the underlying metal and intend to ride the momentum with my PAAS position.

data.title: Astera Labs: Why 2026 Could Be The Breakout Year
data.description:
The AI trade is moving into a more selective phase. The early rush to secure compute is fading, and the focus is shifting toward efficiency, optimization, and system design. In this environment, Astera Labs (ALAB) is no longer just riding AI growth. It is becoming a critical layer in how modern data centers are built.

Astera does not compete with GPU vendors. It solves the bottlenecks they create by moving data reliably across dense, multi-accelerator systems without excessive latency or power loss. As AI clusters grow larger, this capability moves from a performance boost to a basic requirement. That shift is the core reason the Astera Labs thesis works.

Operating Results Indicate Structural Demand, Not Tactical Pull-Forward

The latest financials of Astera Labs indicate that the demand is more structural than cyclical. The firm recorded revenue of $230.6 million for the third quarter of 2025, registering a yearly growth of 104% and a sequential boost of 20%. The most prominent feature of the quarter is the profitability achievement, wherein the firm recorded a record non-GAAP operating margin of 41.7%.

These metrics are important as they show that the expansion is being realized in conjunction with operating leverage. The company is not scaling on the back of pricing or pulse demand, but rather on expanded content per platform, as well as across their product offerings. This differentiates Astera from peer companies that were scaling on the back of margin expansion or single-product cycles.

The data also indicates Astera has moved beyond an inflection point from a niche player in supplying to become a scaled infrastructure player. Industry standards for revenue magnitude, margins, and engagement characteristics reflect those found in mature platforms in the semiconductor industry, not start-up component suppliers.

Product Mix Is Shifting Toward Higher Strategic Value

Historically, the business has been driven by Aries PCIe retimer products, but going forward, the company’s valuation is less dependent on the retimer business and more dependent on the scale-up business and the Scorpio family of products.

It is a significant change. Retimers add to the signal range, while scale-up switches set up communication between accelerators in a rack. Control over the switching fabric puts Astera further into system architecture and significantly boosts the dollar value for every rack.

According to management, Scorpio X-Series products are set to enter production systems late in 2025, though revenue will grow more substantially in 2026. By contrast, while new products may see major improvements, scaled-up fabrics enter a new generation more likely to remain in use through several system architectures, giving revenue visibility that is longer term in nature.

This trend towards scale-up architectures also has ties to overall trends within the design of AI systems. With larger models being deployed along with more distributed workloads, all-to-all communication between accelerators is becoming more relevant. This is what scale-up architectures help address.

The positioning of Astera in this context is interesting in that they are not tied in with a particular accelerator supplier. This allows support for either merchant GPUs or custom accelerators. This kind of flexibility is becoming important as hyperscalers are now looking to in-house solutions in addition to third-party solutions.

Open Standards Offer Strategic Optionality

Astera’s adherence to standards such as PCIe and UALink makes it different from competitors constrained by their own, closed interconnect architectures. In fact, UALink was created to go beyond what could be accomplished in memory semantic connectivity using the existing PCIe architecture.

Expectations are for UALink-based offerings to start emerging in the market during the latter half of 2026, with initial revenue streams expected from 2027. More significantly, it is intended that UALink will be complementary, rather than competitive, with PCIe technology. This enables customers to take advantage of improved performance without requiring new software stack implementations.

This helps minimize binary risk. Astera is not relying on any one standard being dominant for success. It is helped by the increased adoption of scale up connectivity that is independent of protocol.

Preparing for the Optical Inflection Point

The development of custom connectivity solutions such as NVLink Fusion designs has emerged as a response to the increasing need for heterogeneous AI systems. Inhomogeneous computing systems require interconnects that are no longer standard.

By participating in custom connectivity solutions, Astera’s influence is not just contained within traditional sockets. This usually enhances the content that accelerators can handle because of the added complexity. Customization carries risks with regard to execution but amplifies the target market beyond traditional components.

From a strategic viewpoint, customized connectivity helps to further position Astera as an infrastructure supplier rather than just a component supplier. Although copper interconnections are adequate for presently envisioned RAS configurations, eventually physical limitations will force the use of optical interconnections. This is due to increasing power densities within the RAS rack, as well as the rise of multi-rack clusters.

The acquisition by Astera of Xscale Photonics puts the company in a position to capitalize on this transition when it happens, which will likely happen in 2028 or 2029. The optical interconnect market can be seen as a long-term play, not an immediate revenue maker. The company is getting a head start by aligning with customer roadmaps.

Most importantly, the emphasis has been on flexibility regarding the integration of photonics. Instead of promoting an internal solution of choice, Astera plans to accommodate various photonic technologies depending on customer choice.

Valuation Reflects Expectations of Continued Execution

At current levels, Astera Labs screens as optically expensive, trading at roughly 33–34x forward sales and north of 90x forward non-GAAP earnings. On static metrics, that valuation leaves little margin for error and clearly prices in a successful execution of the scale-up roadmap. However, focusing only on today’s multiples misses the speed and quality of the underlying earnings reset.

Consensus estimates a revenue increase from around $830 million in FY2025 to $1.18 billion in FY2026, and subsequent growth to over $2 billion in 2028. If such is seen to happen, forward PS ratio will promptly move into the low 20s in 2026 and into the low teens in 2028 without requiring a multiple expansion. Importantly, this growth is paired with ~40%+ operating margins, placing Astera closer to infrastructure software economics than traditional hardware suppliers.

The market is effectively valuing ALAB as a future architectural control point rather than a component vendor. That valuation is demanding, but defensible, provided Scorpio X ramps on schedule and hyperscaler deployments remain intact.

What Could Go Wrong

The risk for Astera does not relate to technological obsolescence or irrelevance but execution risk and customer concentration. Scale-up technology requires complex integration. Delays are expected, so valuation does not leave much room for error.

Furthermore, the spending trends among the hyperscalers may be irregular. Even a brief interruption in spending may affect the visibility of revenues. Even though the long cycle times reduce the risk of sudden displacements, there may be fluctuations in the short term.

Competition from larger semiconductor companies and their diversified product offerings is also a factor that needs to be considered. Bundling may impact such decisions where cost optimization is of prime importance.

Bottom Line

Astera Labs is undergoing a transformation to assume a structurally significant role in AI infrastructure. With the rising complexity of systems and the role of connectivity in defining system performance, solutions offered by the company inch towards the architectural heart of AI clusters.

The outlook is based on continued support for scaled architectures, successful execution of product line ramps, and continued hyperscaler spending. Although valuation embeds high expectations, Astera’s positioning, margins, and growing importance in AI systems offer a solid basis for staying relevant in the long term.

This is no longer a story of incrementing demand for components. This is a story of control points in the emerging stack for AI infrastructure.

data.title: Universal: The Yield Is Attractive But Don't Expect Growth
data.description:
Universal Corporation (UVV) is a small importer-exporter operating in a stagnant industry. That said, dividend growth investors may know the company because of its status as a Dividend King. Despite the attractive 6% yield and 55-year streak of annual dividend increases, the firm is the middleman in a declining industry. Granted, Universal is expanding its non-tobacco operations, but the segment is still minor with inconsistent profitability. I am not convinced the company will reach a profitable scale in this segment in the near future. Moreover, the dividend safety metrics are not strong, and the balance sheet is leveraged. Hence, I view Universal as a sell.

Overview of Universal

Universal Corporation was founded in 1886 and is headquartered in Richmond, VA. The company is the global leader in the import and export of tobacco leaf. It sources tobacco leaf from farmers and sells processed products to cigarette and other manufacturers. The firm also performs a similar function for plant-based ingredients and packaged foods companies. The Tobacco Operations segment accounts for about 89% of revenue, while the Ingredients Operations segment accounts for about 11%.

Total revenue was over $2,947 million in fiscal 2024 and $2,987 million in the last twelve months ("LTM"). The fiscal year ends on the last day of March.

Latest Results

Universal announced third-quarter fiscal year 2025 results on November 5 th, 2025, beating earnings estimates. Revenue increased 6% to $754.2M from $710.8M year-over-year. Diluted adjusted earnings per share ("EPS") decreased 6% to $1.36 from $1.45 the prior year, but beat estimates by $0.02. Diluted GAAP EPS rose 32% to $1.36 from $1.03.

Both segments showed organic top line growth due to customer demand and higher volumes, but operating income declined. The Tobacco Operations segment reported 5% higher organic sales but 16% lower operating income, because of foreign exchange, write-downs, and a less favorable product mix. The Ingredients Operations segment had 18% higher volumes but an operating income loss due to weaker demand, tariff uncertainty, write-downs, and higher fixed costs. A higher diluted share count was a headwind for earnings.

Consensus estimates are $4.74 per share for fiscal 2025. After the results were released, the share price rose slightly. The share price has decreased 1.8% year-to-date ("YTD") and is 1.1% lower over the LTM.

Declining Industry and Leverage

Tobacco Usage is Declining

Smoking is a declining industry in the United States. According to the American Lung Association's analysis of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention data, cigarette smoking rates have decreased 73% to 11.2% of adults in 2022 from 42.6% of adults in 1965. Youth cigarette smoking has fallen even more in percentage terms, at an 82% decline to 3.8% in 2022 from 36.4% in 1997. Moreover, the number of cigarettes being smoked per day among current smokers has fallen, continuing long-term trends. Similarly, the data show a general decline in the use of cigars, pipe tobacco, and smokeless tobacco from 2002 to 2022 across the adult and youth categories. A similar, though less dramatic, decline is occurring in other countries as well.

The combination of regulation and higher state taxes will likely continue to pressure usage. Most countries and states have restrictions on marketing and advertising, require packaging warnings, and limit smoking in public spaces. Although cigarette manufacturers have remained highly profitable, this has occurred through price increases and consolidation, not organic volume growth. The outcome is that lower usage suggests less demand for tobacco leaf.

Expansion into Ingredients is Inconsistent

Universal has tried to address long-term trends by expanding into non-tobacco products, specifically plant-based ingredients, in the United States. The firm acquired three established companies (Silva International, FruitSmart, and Shank's Extracts) that import, export, process, and package fresh and dehydrated plant-based ingredients for packaged food companies. Overall, this diversification move was justified because of parallels with the tobacco business model.

However, Universal is not an established player in the space, and the customers are not cigarette manufacturers, but packaged food companies. The Ingredients Operations segment now accounts for about 11% of total revenue, but it is not consistently profitable. In the most recent quarter, operating income was negative. Although Universal could expand further through M&A, it has not attempted to do so. Instead, the focus has been on organic growth to increase scale. The main reason could be the lack of financial flexibility afforded by the leveraged balance sheet.

Too Much Leverage Limits Flexibility

The balance sheet is leveraged, with a considerable amount of net debt, limiting financial flexibility. At the end of the second quarter of fiscal year 2025, Universal held $88.7 million in cash, offset by $539.6 million in short-term and current long-term debt and $618.2 million in long-term debt. The leverage ratio is 3.32X, while interest coverage is 3.29X. Neither value is optimal for a company in a mature and declining industry. The credit rating agencies agree and have issued a BBB- lower-medium investment-grade rating with a negative outlook. Moreover, the firm had challenges generating positive free cash flow ("FCF") in fiscal 2022 and 2023, which required additional debt. The situation reversed itself in 2024, but it points to operating difficulties.

Notably, net debt and leverage were moderate until the acquisitions and expansion into the Ingredients Operations business. However, the company paid cash to complete the transactions and has used short-term borrowing to fund operations. At the same time, the dividend distribution increased by 30% in 2019, placing greater demand on FCF. Consequently, the Universal's financial flexibility to expand the Ingredients Operations business by M&A is limited. It could slash the dividend, but that would affect the Dividend King status and yield, and the primary reasons why investors are interested in this equity. In hindsight, the firm should have issued a special dividend instead of providing such a dramatic increase.

Recent Challenges and Risks

Universal's main risk is the long-term secular decline of smoking, which is a global phenomenon. This is unlikely to reverse itself. Along these lines, additional regulation and higher retail taxes would curtail demand for its main products.

Next, tariffs are a persistent risk. The firm sources tobacco from many countries, which are subject to rapidly changing tariff rates due to ad hoc policies. Management has also identified tariff uncertainty as impacting operating income.

Another risk is that Universal could overpay for an acquisition. This would lead to impairment charges if growth and expansion plans do not materialize.

Competitive Advantages

Universal has the advantage of being one of the two global suppliers in sourcing, processing, and selling tobacco leaf. The firm has significant expertise in sourcing tobacco leaf and managing the supply chain in areas with limited infrastructure.

The firm does not need to make significant capital investment due to the nature of its end markets. Also, competition is limited because few companies will invest in a declining industry facing secular decline.

Dividend Analysis

Now, let's analyze Universal's dividend. Since the increase in 2019, the dividend yield has fluctuated around 6%. The forward yield is about 6.05%, near the 4-year average of about 6%. This indicates a fairly valued stock.

Universal is a Dividend King with a 55-year streak of consecutive yearly increases. Over the last 5 years, the growth rate was ~1.3%. Over the trailing decade, the growth rate was greater at approximately 4.6%, driven by the large increase in 2019. Since then, the increases have been $0.01 per share. The most recent quarterly increase was for 1.2% to $0.82 per share in May 2025. The payout ratio is elevated at roughly 70%, which is above my desired value of 65%.

Acceptable, but a few concerning safety metrics support the dividend. The forward payout ratio is 70%, based on an estimated consensus 2025 EPS of $4.74. This value is below my target value of 65% or better. I expect the ratio to remain elevated because of inconsistent EPS growth and incremental distribution increases. The firm's FCF is also volatile and does not always cover the dividend each year. However, the FCF of $264 million covered the dividend payout of around $80 million in fiscal 2024. Assuming $160 million of FCF and $85 million in dividends this year, because of an increase and a higher share count, gives a dividend-to-FCF ratio of ~53%, a conservative value and less than my target of 70% or better.

Universal's balance sheet is leveraged, and interest coverage is relatively low. The company has $539.6 million of short-term and current long-term debt. It has $618.2 million in long-term debt. It holds about $88.7 million in cash, equivalents, and short-term securities. The leverage ratio is 3.32X, while interest coverage is 3.29X. Notably, interest expense has increased from around $49 million in fiscal 2023 to $80 million in fiscal 2025 due to more short-term and higher short-term rates. The credit rating agencies awarded Universal a BBB- lower-medium investment grade rating with a negative outlook, which is concerning.

Although Universal's dividend safety metrics are not ideal, the dividend is currently safe, and I do not expect a near-term reduction. I expect distributions to increase at a low single-digit rate for the next several years based on the payout ratio and leverage.

Valuation

Now, let's analyze Universal's valuation. Its current share price and projected 2025 EPS have given the equity a low valuation compared to the broader market. The stock is valued at ~11.4 times fiscal 2025 earnings.

Consensus estimates for diluted earnings are $4.74 per share for fiscal 2025, up from $ 4.63 in fiscal 2024. We will use 12X as a realistic, fair-value multiple, which is near the 10-year average. I remain concerned about Universal's growth, inconsistent operating income, leverage, and outlook. As a result, my fair value estimate is $56.88. The current share price is ~$53.84, suggesting Universal is slightly undervalued.

Applying a sensitivity analysis using P/E ratios of 11X to 13X, we obtain a fair value range of $52.14 to $61.62. Hence, the stock price is approximately 87% to 103% of the fair value estimate.

According to Seeking Alpha, Wall Street analysts do not cover this stock. The Seeking Alpha Quant system rating is a hold because of unfavorable growth and profitability. However, valuation, momentum, and revisions are favorable.

Final Thoughts

Universal is a stock that income investors may want to like. However, I have concerns about the secular decline in smoking and tobacco use affecting demand, the challenging expansion into fresh and dehydrated plant-based ingredients, and the balance sheet. Granted, the Dividend King status and yield are attractive. However, the safety metrics are not consistently good, and the distribution growth rate is exceptionally low. I do not expect either to change in the near term. In fact, I expect the share price, valuation, and yield to remain relatively constant. Hence, given better stock opportunities in the dividend growth space, I view Universal as a sell.
